nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving blackwater streams, peat swamps, and densely vegetated floodplain pools with tannin-stained, soft, acidic water and leaf-litter substrate

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, flame moss, floating plants)
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) for tannins and refuge
driftwood to lower pH and add visual complexity
dark substrate to enhance coloration and reduce fish stress

diet.notes: Micropredator requiring tiny foods — live or frozen micro-fare such as Moina, baby brine shrimp, micro worms (Panagrellus), Daphnia, vinegar eels, and infusoria. Accepts high-quality micro-pellets (0.3–0.5 mm) and finely powdered flake as supplemental feeding. Mouth is very small; standard flake or pellets are too large. Varied diet enhances coloration and conditioning for breeding.

goodWith: other Boraras species (B. brigittae, B. urophthalmoides)
small Rasbora species of similar size
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that may outcompete for food
fish large enough to consume Boraras as prey

summary: One of the world’s smallest aquarium fish, Boraras micros reaches only ~2 cm and displays three distinctive black spots along its body. A Thai blackwater specialist requiring soft, acidic, tannin-rich water and tiny micro-prey; shrimp-safe and ideal for well-planted nano biotope tanks.
careNotes: Boraras micros is among the smallest cyprinids kept in the aquarium hobby, with adults rarely exceeding 2 cm. The species originates from soft, acidic blackwater habitats in Thailand, and replicating these conditions is essential for long-term health and natural behaviour. Water should be very soft (gH 1–6, kH 0–3) and acidic (pH 4.0–6.5); hard or alkaline tap water should be pre-treated using reverse osmosis or aged peat filtration. Adding Indian almond (catappa) leaves and driftwood leaches tannins into the water column, lowering pH, releasing humic acids, and approximating the species’ native biotope. A dark substrate and subdued lighting are strongly recommended — bright conditions cause the fish to hide and lose their vivid pigmentation. Filtration must provide gentle flow; a sponge filter is ideal as standard power filters create too much current and pose a fry-loss risk. A minimum group of 8 individuals is advisable to bring out schooling behaviour and reduce individual stress; larger groups of 15–20 produce more impressive displays. Because the species is extremely small, tankmate selection is critical — only similarly sized, peaceful fish and dwarf shrimp should be housed alongside them. Boraras micros is considered shrimp-safe: adult dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) are not at risk, though newly moulted juveniles may occasionally be investigated. The species is intermediate in difficulty due to its strict water chemistry requirements, small mouth necessitating specialised feeding, and sensitivity to poor water quality.
breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers that spawn among fine-leaved plants and moss. No parental care is provided and adults may eat eggs if not removed. Conditioning breeding adults on live micro-prey (Moina, micro worms) for 1–2 weeks markedly improves spawning success. Spawning is typically triggered by a small (10–15%) water change with slightly cooler, very soft acidic water (pH 5.0–5.5, gH ≤2). Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; fry are extremely tiny and require infusoria or commercial liquid fry food for the first week before graduating to micro worms and baby brine shrimp nauplii. A species-only breeding tank of 15–20 L densely planted with Java moss is the most reliable setup. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to water-chemistry precision required and tiny-fry rearing demands.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Boraras micros
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~2 cm), temperature range 22–28°C, pH 4.0–6.5, hardness 1–6 dGH, native range (Thailand), blackwater biotope, diet (micropredator), compatibility, and breeding notes. Tank minimum and decor guidance cross-checked against FishBase and Aquarium Co-Op.

additional.name: FishBase — Boraras micros
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 1.6 cm SL, approximately 2 cm TL), taxonomy (Cyprinidae, Cypriniformes), native range (Thailand — Chao Phraya and Mae Klong drainages), temperature (22–28°C), and diet classification (omnivore, micropredator of zooplankton).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Chili Rasbora and Nano Fish Care
additional.notes: Supplemental source for general Boraras husbandry, nano-tank setup recommendations, water parameter guidance for soft-water Southeast Asian fish, and compatible tankmate lists. No species-specific Boraras micros page available; general Boraras/nano-fish guidance applied.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Boraras species guide
additional.notes: Supplemental source covering the Boraras genus, care requirements, distinguishing B. micros from B. brigittae by the three-spot pattern (two lateral spots plus one near the caudal peduncle), blackwater biotope requirements, group size recommendations (10+), and breeding overview.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Boraras micros species profile
additional.notes: Supplemental source confirming three-spot identification, Thai origin (Chao Phraya drainage), soft-acidic blackwater requirements, micropredator diet, shrimp compatibility, and nano biotope tank suitability. Breeding notes (egg-scattering, Java moss, fry rearing) confirmed against SeriouslyFish primary.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterers spawning in dense Java moss; adults eat eggs and must be removed post-spawn. Requires very soft, acidic conditioning water and live micro-prey to trigger spawning. Fry extremely tiny — require infusoria for first week.
